topic: Mamdani Arrest Netanyahu Enforce Warrant Mayor

left.title: Mamdani says he can’t arrest Netanyahu as mayor and urges US to enforce ICC warrant

left-center.title: Mamdani: NYC Doesn’t Have ‘Legal Authority’ To Enforce ICC Arrest Warrant Against Netanyahu

farLeftText: Zohran Mamdani, the New York City mayor, doubled down on his support for the international criminal court (ICC) on Wednesday and called on federal authorities to execute the court’s warrant for Benjamin Netanyahu’s arrest should he visit the city in the fall. “I call on the federal government to join the international criminal court and to execute both this warrant as well as any warrant the international criminal court holds,” Mamdani told reporters at a press conference on Wednesday. Those remarks came less than 24 hours after Mamdani called the Israeli prime minister a “war criminal” who is “not welcome in New York City” in a video statement posted on social media. In the statement, the mayor acknowledged he had no legal authority to direct New York police to arrest Netanyahu should he attend the United Nations general assembly in September. Mamdani had previously said he was “in conversation” with city lawyers to determine whether his administration could arrest Netanyahu, as he had pledged to do during his mayoral campaign last year. On Wednesday, Mamdani rejected arguments from critics that Israel’s war crimes in Gaza have nothing to do with New York City. Israel’s actions in Gaza, he said, had “offended the conscience, the morals, the mere humanity of so many New Yorkers”. Mamdani’s statements about Netanyahu were remarkable for a US municipal leader and a signal of how support for Palestinian rights remains a signature policy for him. His insistence that the US government arrest Netanyahu has been met with condemnation and ridicule from pro-Israel officials. Pro-Palestinian advocates’ reactions were more mixed – some praised the mayor’s Tuesday statement for its “moral clarity” while others expressed disappointment that he walked back on his promise to seek the Israeli leader’s arrest. “I think the mayor should stay in his lane,” Jonathan Harounoff, Israel’s former international spokesperson at the United Nations, told NY1, accusing him of “political theater”. John Fetterman of Pennsylvania, one of the most staunchly pro-Israel Democrats in the Senate, called Mamdani a “clown” who should “sit down”. “America’s not even part of that corrupt court,” Fetterman said in an interview with Fox News over the weekend. “That’s really not your purview.” Simcha Eichenstein, a pro-Israel New York state assemblymember, had previously called on the state attorney general to review whether Mamdani’s comments about Netanyahu violated state law. The ICC issued arrest warrants against Netanyahu and the former Israeli defense minister Yoav Gallant in 2024, accusing them of war crimes and crimes against humanity, including “starvation as a method of warfare, and … murder, persecution, and other inhumane acts”. “I believe Prime Minister Netanyahu belongs in The Hague,” Mamdani told the New York Times last week, referring to the city in the Netherlands that is home to the court. Neither the US nor Israel are members of the ICC, which the US government has long opposed and which Marco Rubio, the secretary of state, this month threatened to “dismantle” by pressuring other countries to abandon it. Mamdani said he concluded his administration had no authority to execute the ICC arrest warrant, but shifted the burden on the Trump administration to do so. skip past newsletter promotion Free newsletter | Around 2-3 times a day Sign up to Breaking News US Get the most important news as it breaks Preview latest Enter your email Sign up after newsletter promotion “It is clear that we do not have the independent legal authority to enforce this warrant,” he said in Tuesday’s statement. “The federal government, however, does – and I call on them to join the ICC and execute this warrant.” Donald Trump has already said he will do no such thing. Netanyahu “will not be arrested” on US soil, the president said in a social media post earlier this week, adding that the Israeli prime minister was “fighting against the Islamic Republic of Iran” and “the only ones that should be arrested are the people that led Iran into this unprecedented spiral of death and destruction”. Netanyahu had said in the past he was “not concerned” with Mamdani’s threat, and his office said in a statement last week that the mayor “appears interested in diverting public attention from his follies and attacking the leader of the Jewish state and the only democracy in the Middle East”. It called the ICC a “kangaroo court”. Supporters of the court have also praised Mamdani’s efforts to stand up for the tribunal’s mission. “My mayor is doing what any NYC mayor would do if Vladimir Putin or any other indicted war criminal planned to visit NYC,” said Adil Haque, a legal scholar. “He is exploring every legal avenue and exhausting all legal options. If his hands are tied, he will use his voice.”
centerLeftText: LOADING ERROR LOADING Mayor Zohran Mamdani on Tuesday acknowledged the limitations of New York City’s legal authority when it comes to enforcing an arrest warrant against Israeli Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu, but noted that the federal government doesn’t face the same constraints. “My administration has reviewed every avenue available under applicable law to determine whether New York City can execute the International Criminal Court’s arrest warrant if Benjamin Netanyahu came here. It is clear that we do not have the independent legal authority to enforce this warrant,” Mamdani said in a video posted to X. “The federal government, however, does, and I call on them to join the ICC and execute this warrant.” Advertisement During his mayoral campaign, Mamdani pledged to order Netanyahu’s arrest, and he recently told The New York Times that he was in active discussions with his office’s legal department about what the law would allow him to do. In the wake of that interview, President Donald Trump vowed that the Israeli leader, who could visit New York City this fall for the U.N. General Assembly, would not be arrested in the U.S. in “any way, shape, or form.” Benjamin Netanyahu is a war criminal. pic.twitter.com/YRezmW6YVx — Mayor Zohran Kwame Mamdani (@NYCMayor) July 22, 2026 Advertisement In 2024, the ICC issued an arrest warrant for Netanyahu and accused him of crimes against humanity, which he denied. That warrant stated that Netanyahu was “allegedly responsible for the war crimes of starvation as a method of warfare and of intentionally directing an attack against the civilian population” as part of Israel’s military campaign against Gaza. The U.S. is not a party to the Rome Statute, which established the ICC, and does not observe its authority. The Trump administration has also taken a more hostile posture toward the ICC, with Secretary of State Marco Rubio recently launching a campaign aimed at dismantling the body. Mamdani, who described Netanyahu as “the architect of a horrific genocide against the Palestinian people,” also detailed why the prime minister should be arrested. Advertisement “He is responsible for the killing of more than 73,000 people, for the maiming of tens of thousands of children, as those who survive undergo amputation without anesthesia. For the targeting of neonatal hospitals and maternity care centers, denying newborns even the chance to live. For the countless people he starved as he blocked food and humanitarian aid from reaching them. For the gunning down of hundreds of aid workers and journalists,” Mamdani alleged in his video on Tuesday. “And just last month, the U.N. confirmed that Palestinian children continue to be deliberately targeted and killed by Israeli armed forces more than eight months after the so-called ceasefire.” “I agree with the ICC that Benjamin Netanyahu should be arrested and tried for his crimes, as I do for anyone else charged by the ICC,” Mamdani added. In a statement on Sunday, Netanyahu’s office attacked the ICC, calling it a “kangaroo court,” and criticized Mamdani in response. It also claimed that Israel had “taken unprecedented wartime measures to minimize harm to civilians while confronting Hamas.”

topic: Cockroach Protests Indias Modi Promises Punish

left.title: ‘Cockroach’ protests: India’s Modi promises to punish officials over exam leaks

center.title: India's Modi government under growing pressure as 'cockroach' protests intensify

farLeftText:
Narendra Modi has vowed to set up “fast-track courts” to speed up the punishment of officials accused of leaking examination papers, as the Indian prime minister seeks to quell a youth-led “cockroach” protest movement that has rocked Delhi and posed one of the biggest challenges to his government.

In his first public remarks on the movement, Modi posted on X: “Nothing is more important than the welfare and future of our youth … Those who try to harm the future of our youth will not be spared.”

The offer of faster justice than usual from India’s clogged judicial system, which can take decades to deliver a verdict, was swiftly rejected by the Cockroach Janta party (CJP), the satirical name the youth protest movement coined after India’s top judge called unemployed youths “cockroaches”.

View image in fullscreen Demonstrators carrying the Indian national flag at a march in Patna. Photograph: Sachin Kumar/AFP/Getty Images

Saurav Das, a CJP spokesperson, said: “It’s a Band-aid. That’s not giving us accountability. There have been zero convictions so far all these years, so this measure is of no use.” A core demand of the movement is the resignation of the education minister, Dharmendra Pradhan, as well as reform of the education system.

An estimated 50,000 people protested in Delhi on Monday, after police denied them permission to march on parliament. Officers later mounted baton charges and fired teargas. By Wednesday, more than 1,000 people remained camped at the CJP’s protest site in central Delhi despite the gruelling heat and humidity. Many had travelled long distances to be there.

Renuka Chowdhury, an opposition MP with the Congress party, told the news channel India Today that no one could fathom why Modi would not sack Pradhan. “Honestly, is this one minister more valuable or more important than restoring harmony and order to the country?” he said.

Modi’s statement follows signs the government may be softening its stance. Ministers have insisted they are willing to talk to the CJP but have refused to discuss Pradhan’s resignation.

View image in fullscreen Volunteers distribute food received to protesters. Photograph: Vipin/AP

The protest site at Jantar Mantar has drawn crowds of supporters eager to show their solidarity. Unable to fit inside the enclosed area, many have spilled on to the surrounding roads. Some organise donations of food and water sent by wellwishers, while others distribute meals – including to the police on duty – fan those wilting from the heat or collect litter.

Young people across India have also rallied behind the CJP’s demands, with three consecutive days of demonstrations in Mumbai and solidarity protests in Chennai, Indore, Hyderabad and Patiala.

At a press conference on Wednesday, the opposition leader Rahul Gandhi escalated his criticism, demanding that in addition to Pradhan resigning, Modi should apologise for failing to provide a fair education system.

Gandhi said there had been 152 exam paper leaks across the country over the past decade without a single conviction.

“Somebody, or a group of people, are destroying our education system, India’s most valuable asset, destroying the lives of our students. What does the government do? Zero convictions,” he said.

The health minister, JP Nadda, accused Gandhi of exploiting the protests for political gain.

View image in fullscreen Demonstrators hold anti-Modi placards during a protest. Photograph: Ashish Vaishnav/Sopa Images/Shutterstock

“Your concern is not to improve the education system, enhance the quality of education or ensure justice for students. Instead, you are trying to use students’ issues and their demands for political mileage. You are trying to derive political benefit from them, and that is unfortunate,” he said.

CJP leaders were deeply disappointed after Monday’s meeting with Nadda – the first in over 60 days – ended without a breakthrough. Its founder, Abhijeet Dipke, has since said that if ministers want to speak to them, they will have to come to the protest site and do so in public.

Many in India are asking why Pradhan will not resign. Anish Gawande, an opposition politician, said Modi viewed backing down as a sign of weakness, particularly now that he no longer commands the parliamentary majority he enjoyed during his first two terms.

“He is facing global headwinds that prevent him from executing any major reforms in agriculture, energy, the economy. The unemployment rate is climbing to levels not seen in three decades,” he said. “A show of weakness now will give an entry point for the opposition to move in and build a similar momentum around other issues […] Before you know it, things have spiralled out of control.”
centerLeftText: No article found in this category for this topic.
centerText: On Monday, tens of thousands of protesters flooded central Delhi after the CJP called for a march to parliament as the monsoon session began. At least 60 people were injured after police used batons and tear gas to disperse the crowds. Officers also dismantled the stage and makeshift tents at Jantar Mantar, the protest's main base near the 300-year-old observatory. But the demonstrators have refused to leave, maintaining a round-the-clock sit-in. A day after the violence, they were visited by several senior opposition leaders such as former Delhi chief minister Arvind Kejriwal and veteran politician Sharad Pawar. Later on Tuesday, Congress president Mallikarjun Kharge and Gandhi led a surprise march to Modi's house, demanding the resignations of Pradhan, Home Minister Amit Shah who the Delhi police report to and PM Modi. Gandhi said the government must apologise to students for the crackdown, adding that the Congress decided to stage a protest outside Modi's house because they weren't allowed to raise the issue in the parliament. He said he had met speaker Om Birla to seek a discussion, but "the speaker said he needed to take permission from the government, but it was very clear to us that there was no interest in the government of having a debate". Federal Minister Jitendra Singh denied Gandhi's allegation, external - he said he informed the Congress MP that the government had accepted his demand for a discussion but alleged that Gandhi later also demanded the resignation of Pradhan. He accused Gandhi of "changing the goalpost" and of refusing to end the protest despite being told that the place wasn't conducive to such demonstrations.

topic: Iran School Killed Strike Trump Images

left.title: Trump Says Images of Bombed Iran School Could Be "AI" But Study Confirms U.S. Killed 140+ in Strike

center.title: 'What was their crime?': BBC visits Iran school where strike killed 120 children

farLeftText:
This is a rush transcript. Copy may not be in its final form.

AMY GOODMAN: The Trump administration has repeatedly refused to take responsibility for the school strike. President Trump recently appeared on Fox News and was questioned by Trey Yingst about the strike.

TREY YINGST: Democrats released a letter calling on your administration to unveil the findings of the investigation into a strike that killed more than a hundred children on the first day of the war. Will you commit to releasing the findings of this investigation?

PRESIDENT DONALD TRUMP: Well, I’d have to speak to the generals that are doing the — because we mostly have generals. You know, I don’t think anybody’s going to ever be able to say what happened there.

TREY YINGST: There are images from this site that show fragments that appear to be from U.S. Tomahawk missiles. Is it possible that old intelligence or a mistake during a very active time in the war led to this event?

PRESIDENT DONALD TRUMP: It is, but it’s also possible that those images that you have are AI-generated. … Now, I know we’re waiting for a conclusive report. I don’t think there can be a conclusive report. There are missiles —

TREY YINGST: Will you release the report when it is finished?

PRESIDENT DONALD TRUMP: I guess so. I mean, I have to ask the military people. There might be a reason why they can’t.

AMY GOODMAN: While President Trump claimed evidence of the school strike may be AI-generated, Forensic Architecture is out with a new report revealing new details about the U.S. bombing. Joining us from London will be a Forensic Architecture researcher. But first, Sky News recently released its own major report on the school strike, based in part on the research of Forensic Architecture. One mother, Khadija Ahmedizadeh, spoke to Sky News about her son Mohammad Taha, who died in the attack.

KHADIJA AHMEDIZADEH: [translated] I collapsed onto the ground on my hands and knees. I said, “Mohammad Taha will choke. For God’s sake, pull him out quickly.” I picked up a stone and said, “I’ll kill myself with this if you don’t bring my son out, quickly, before he chokes!”

It was one month after. We were told that Mohammad Taha had been found. I thought they would show him to me. But when we went, I saw that a child I had raised for 10 years with so much effort was now reduced to the size of a newborn baby, wrapped in a shroud. They gave him to me and said, “Here, this is your child.” I didn’t see my child’s face again.

After one month, Mohammad Taha was laid to rest next to his classmates. I spent that whole night at the cemetery. I was scared. Mohammad Taha was afraid of the dark, too.

They tore my son to pieces. What should I do now? Who am I supposed to hold responsible for this? Who do I have any power over?

AMY GOODMAN: We’re joined now in London by Samaneh Moafi, assistant director of research at Forensic Architecture, the new report titled “Airstrikes on Minab Primary School.”

This is very painful, Samaneh, because we’re talking about real life. We’re talking about well over 160 children and some adults who were killed on that first day when the U.S. struck Minab. You also hear President Trump saying, “We don’t know actually what happened,” as he’s questioned by Fox, and it may be “AI-generated.” You have done the research. What exactly did you find?

SAMANEH MOAFI: I want to take one step before, and I want to talk about where Minab is. Minab is in the south of Iran along the Strait of Hormuz. The war in 2026 started from the very site where the Iran-Iraq War was brought to an end. In 1988, the United States had escalated its military operations against the oil infrastructure and naval bases in Iran. And amongst those strikes, it also shut down an Iranian passenger flight by the Strait of Hormuz and attributed it to a misidentification. All 290 passengers on that flight were killed. This is why, for many Iranians, the attack on Shajareh Tayyebeh primary school echoed the downing of that passenger flight 38 years earlier. It was a nation’s trauma being relived. Some of the preliminary reports had said that the attack on the school, like the passenger flight, was the result of a misidentification, a targeting mistake.

In our investigation, we used satellite imagery to show that at the time of the attack, the building was a school and had served that purpose for over a decade. By October 2015, all architectural indicators of military presence, a watchtower or anything, were destroyed. And new entrances were built. New perimeter walls were built around the school. By 2017, we see a football pitch and assembly lines being painted on the playgrounds. By 2018, we see the walls being painted in blue and pink to identify the girls’ school and the boys’ school. We see children playing in the playgrounds of these — in the satellite imagery. We were able to verify also that many of the buildings in the immediate surrounding of the school were visibly civilian — a clinic, a cultural center, a cafe, a supermarket, a car wash. Many of these facilities maintained active social media accounts, so they were publishing and posting about what is happening in them. Some of these were used by the school. The culture center was — offered space for children for their ceremonies, award ceremonies, so on and so forth.

It’s true that, you know, during the Iran-Iraq War, this site was used as a military base. In the 1980s, CIA documents also refer to a possible military support facility in Minab, laying the ground kind of for like a target, you know? But the Sky News documentary that featured this investigation showed that these targets had not been checked properly for years and years.

I can also tell you how further we went. We looked into the photographs and videos that were published on the school’s website, publicly. These were images of ceremonies, say, the Mother’s Day ceremony, the Nowruz celebrations that marked the new year, the book fairs. We use these images and videos to reconstruct the building of the school as a kind of a navigable 3D model. Some of these images belong to January, only a few weeks before the strikes had happened. Some of the children we saw in those images — Mohammad Taha was one of them — were killed during the strike. The digital model that we built went into as much detail as interior finishes of every single classroom, the decorations on the walls, the bright paint on the staircase that linked the assembly hall of the boys’ school on the ground floor to the girls’ school on the top floor. We checked every room. There was no hidden room for any kind of a use. It was a school for — you know, for children.

AMY GOODMAN: And if you can say — apparently, this according to CNN, the investigation into the U.S. strike that hit the school in Iran has sat for months with the military command, while leaders have held off on ordering a critical standard intelligence review. And we also know about the cutting of the agencies that would review the target sites. In this last minute we have, Samaneh Moafi, the most important evidence you felt you had concluding it was a U.S. Tomahawk missile?

SAMANEH MOAFI: We looked at images. There were images and videos available from the time of the strikes. The first nine strikes started at around 11:20 and across one-and-a-half minute. The first strike landed only 130 meters away from the school. The fourth strike landed 10 seconds after on the school. Mohammad Taha, the 9-year-old you were mentioning in the beginning, was playing in the schoolyard when the first strike begins, and he rushes to the safety of his classroom. And that’s when the school is hit.

Videos of the aftermath of these strikes show that families are on site. Aid workers are on site. They’re rescuing the children under the rubble. These images are from 12:30, 1:15 p.m., like around noon. A number of children were rescued at this time. Parastesh Zaeri, the 11-year-old child, was amongst them. From as early as 1:45, or around that time, multiple Iranian news channels are reporting on the attacks. They’re saying this was — there was an attack on a girls’ school. They’re saying there was death toll of five children, there is more coming, so on and so forth.

AMY GOODMAN: We have 30 seconds.

SAMANEH MOAFI: As families and rescuers are on site, a second wave of strikes are hit. And it’s in these ones that we do see the profile of a Tomahawk. And this is owned — the only party within the war that has this missile is the U.S. Following the initial media reportings, the U.S. forces would have had ample opportunity to reassess their initial misidentification before conducting the next round of strikes. But it’s simple: They didn’t.

AMY GOODMAN: Samaneh Moafi, I want to say thank you very much for being with us, assistant director of research at Forensic Architecture. We’ll link to your new report, “Airstrikes on Minab Primary School.”

That does it for our show. I’ll be in Martha’s Vineyard for the screening of Steal This Story, Please!, July 31st. I’m Amy Goodman.

centerText:
The school day had begun as usual, Amaineh says.

Because it was the Islamic holy month of Ramadan, the children gathered inside the prayer hall to recite the Quran, she recalls, before she began teaching her pupils a new letter of the Persian alphabet.

But, as the first attacks of the war unfolded across Iran, she says the headteacher announced the school would be closing, and teachers began contacting parents, urging them to collect their children.

Only five of Amaineh's 15 first-grade pupils were left in her classroom on the upper floor, she says, when she first heard an explosion not far from the school. She says she was in the corridor and rushed back for the children.

"Just as I went to leave, a missile struck the school," says Amaineh. "Something hit my head and my students' heads - we were all sent flying."

Then, she says, thick black smoke filled the classroom: "I couldn't even see the pupils I was holding in my arms. We couldn't breathe."

She says she remembers hearing another explosion, as well as the sounds of screaming, children crying and then the school itself collapsing – "destroyed all at once".

"Blood was pouring from me and the students," she adds.

topic: Todd Blanche Trumps Slush Fund Epstein

left.title: Todd Blanche Is "Not Fit" to Be AG, from Epstein Files to Trump's $1.8B Slush Fund: Rep. Jayapal

left-center.title: Todd Blanche Denies Telling Democrats He ‘Made A Mistake’ With Trump’s Slush Fund

farLeftText: This is a rush transcript. Copy may not be in its final form. NERMEEN SHAIKH: Acting Attorney General Todd Blanche was grilled by senators during his confirmation hearing Wednesday to become attorney general. Blanche previously served as President Trump’s personal lawyer. He’s headed the Justice Department on an acting basis since Trump fired Pam Bondi as attorney general. It’s unclear if Republicans have enough votes to confirm Blanche. During the hearing, outgoing Texas Senator John Cornyn blasted Blanche for his role in proposing a $1.8 billion fund for Trump to give to his allies, including January 6th insurrectionists. Republican Senator John Kennedy of Louisiana questioned Blanche about his ties to Trump. SEN. JOHN KENNEDY: Are you and President Trump friends? ACTING ATTORNEY GENERAL TODD BLANCHE: I’m his lawyer — was his lawyer, and now I’m the deputy attorney general. AMY GOODMAN: “I’m his lawyer — was his lawyer,” Blanche corrected himself. Democratic Senator Dick Durbin criticized Blanche for continuing to act as if he were Trump’s personal attorney. SEN. DICK DURBIN: In less than 18 months at the Department of Justice, you’ve shown you’re still President Trump’s personal attorney. Your tenure can be summed up in the four words you said — quote, “I love you, sir” — to President Trump. This was your response when you were asked what you would say to him. This nation deserves an attorney general who loves the Constitution more than any single president, an attorney general focused on keeping America safe and combating corruption, not satisfying the president’s personal grievances or channeling crypto cash to the administration’s MAGA faithful. AMY GOODMAN: During the Senate hearing, Blanche also faced questions about his handling of the Epstein files, and we’ll hear his comments in a bit. But for more, we’re joined now from Washington, D.C., by Democratic Congressmember Pramila Jayapal of Washington state. She’s just introduced a bill with Senator Cory Booker to protect Epstein survivors whose personal information was improperly disclosed during the Trump’s DOJ’s recent release of the Epstein files, which Blanche was in charge of. But first, we want to go to the overall questioning of and the possibility that the acting or interim attorney general would become the attorney general. Your assessment of Todd Blanche, Congressmember Jayapal? REP. PRAMILA JAYAPAL: Well, my assessment, Amy, is that he is not fit to be attorney general. He slipped and said what is true, that he acts as the president’s personal attorney, not as the attorney general for the people of the United States. This is a very important position, and Donald Trump is putting his personal attorney in because he wants him to do all the things that Donald Trump wants to do: the political persecutions that have been happening under Todd Blanche as acting attorney general, the cover-up of the Epstein files. You have to ask: What is he hiding for the president that he is not willing to come out and actually take responsibility for and prosecute the predators that are present throughout the Epstein ring? The slush fund, you know, that he negotiated, now he says it’s not going to happen. I don’t believe him. I don’t think there’s anything in writing around that. The settlement with the IRS that he negotiated for Donald Trump himself. These are all indications of why he cannot be trusted as the top attorney for the people of the United States. He might be trusted as the top attorney for Donald Trump. That’s not the role of attorney general. He should not be confirmed. NERMEEN SHAIKH: And, Congressmember Jayapal, if you could clarify? There’s been some reporting that suggests that, in fact, this confirmation hearing may be in part just symbolic, because he could, in principle, be acting attorney general for the duration of Trump’s term. REP. PRAMILA JAYAPAL: Well, it’s true that you can stay as an acting. However, I do think that it would be a significant step if the Republicans did not have the votes to confirm him. And, you know, I think that this is something that we need to fix. There is the possibility of people staying if they’ve been confirmed for another position, and then they can stay and transfer that confirmation into a new position. And so, I think there’s work to be done around that. But I do believe that John Cornyn, perhaps Thom Tillis — not clear to me from the questioning — who are now sort of cut loose because they won’t be continuing in this position as senators, they might have the opportunity now to actually do what is right for the country. That is what I’m hoping for, because I think once he is — once they refuse to confirm him, there’s much more opportunity for us to ensure that he doesn’t continue in that position. NERMEEN SHAIKH: And, Congressmember Jayapal, ahead of Todd Blanche’s confirmation hearing, the group World Without Exploitation released this video featuring Epstein survivors calling on senators to reject Blanche’s nomination because of his role in disclosing their personal information, while protecting the names of alleged abusers during the Department of Justice’s release of records related to the Jeffrey Epstein investigation. SURVIVOR 1: My name was released. SURVIVOR 2: My phone number was released. SURVIVOR 3: Where I went to school. SURVIVOR 4: Where I live. SURVIVOR 5: My students can now search me and read about my abuse. SURVIVOR 6: I am an Epstein survivor. SURVIVOR 7: I am an Epstein survivor. SURVIVOR 8: I am an Epstein survivor. SURVIVOR 1: We were exposed. SURVIVOR 7: Nude pictures of victims were released. SURVIVOR 9: Our family’s safety was put at risk. SURVIVOR 10: Now Todd Blanche wants to be the attorney general of the United States. SURVIVOR 2: Todd Blanche had a duty to protect us. SURVIVOR 11: Instead, he protected the people… SURVIVOR 12: …who committed crimes against us. SURVIVOR 3: Thirty years. SURVIVOR 8: Twelve hundred victims. SURVIVOR 7: And 6 million files. SURVIVOR 13: A mountain of evidence. SURVIVOR 4: But Todd Blanche says there are no investigative leads. SURVIVOR 7: Todd Blanche failed us. SURVIVOR 6: He failed victims everywhere. SURVIVOR 8: We deserve better. SURVIVOR 9: Our country deserves better. SURVIVOR 13: Vote no on Todd Blanche for attorney general. SURVIVOR 12: Vote no. SURVIVOR 6: No. SURVIVOR 2: No. SURVIVOR 14: No. SURVIVOR 5: Vote no on Todd Blanche for attorney general. NERMEEN SHAIKH: And several of Epstein’s accusers were in fact seated in the Senate gallery during the hearing. And at the hearing, Illinois Democratic Senator Dick Durbin had a heated exchange with Acting Attorney General Todd Blanche on whether he would commit to personally meeting with Epstein survivors. SEN. DICK DURBIN: There are 10 individuals who were exploited and abused by Mr. Epstein. They are here today. None of them have had a chance to speak to anyone in the department or FBI, though they’ve asked repeatedly. So, can I get your word, under oath, that within the next 30 days you will personally sit down with these 10 victims and hear their case in terms of what needs to be done by the Department of Justice? ACTING ATTORNEY GENERAL TODD BLANCHE: I — Chairman, I appreciate them being here today. I also have somebody from my office who’s spent her entire career working on cases like Mr. Epstein’s. She’s in charge of our task force investigating human trafficking. She’s available to talk to them, as soon as today. SEN. DICK DURBIN: She can sit right next to you. She can sit right next to you when you meet with these survivors. ACTING ATTORNEY GENERAL TODD BLANCHE: I am — I have never said I will not meet with survivors. I am — so — SEN. DICK DURBIN: Will you meet with these 10 survivors? I’m asking you, on the record. ACTING ATTORNEY GENERAL TODD BLANCHE: If they have lawyers, as you know, I’m prohibited from meeting directly with them. SEN. DICK DURBIN: I think you ought to be in the room, because you ought to hear this. You had singular responsibility for these files. There was a delay in meeting the statutory requirement of disclosure. You were involved in that. I think you ought to be part of this. ACTING ATTORNEY GENERAL TODD BLANCHE: I am definitely part of it, and I have been from day one. NERMEEN SHAIKH: And, Congressmember Jayapal, you’ve just introduced a bill with Senator Cory Booker to protect Epstein survivors whose personal information was improperly disclosed during the Trump DOJ’s recent release of the Epstein files. So, if you could talk about what exactly you’ve called for? REP. PRAMILA JAYAPAL: Yes, we just introduced the REDACT Act, and this comes really from the whole experience of the Epstein survivors over the last year and a half under this Trump administration DOJ and Todd Blanche’s overseeing. And you might remember that when Pam Bondi, then the attorney general, was before us in the Judiciary Committee, where I sit, I did the first line of questioning. And the most powerful moment was when I asked the survivors to stand up, and they all stood up behind Pam Bondi, and I asked Pam Bondi to turn and to acknowledge them and to apologize for the fact that the Department of Justice, under her leadership and Todd Blanche’s leadership — he was really the one who was managing the Epstein files; they’re both responsible — they released the names of these survivors. They released personal information. And in some cases, the survivors had not even told their families about the abuse that they had experienced at the hands of these powerful predators and criminals in the Epstein ring. And at the same time that their personal information was released, they also redacted the — Todd Blanche and Pam Bondi redacted the names of the powerful predators, which was clearly — both things were against the Epstein Transparency Act. The damage that was done out of the release of that personal information — their addresses, their phone numbers, pictures of them, all of the details of the abuse that they had suffered — they have not had any way to get recompense for that. And so, the REDACT Act essentially allows survivors a private right of action to sue the Department of Justice, including for monetary damages, because their information was released. I have spent hours and hours and hours with the survivors. I have gotten to know them incredibly well. They are so brave, so courageous. They are taking so many risks in continuing to stand up. They are inspiring people across the country to stand up and speak out about the abuse that other women have experienced at the hands of traffickers and predators. And it is really remarkable to me that to this day they have not been able to sit down with Todd Blanche. And I will just say also that after that hearing, I was invited to meet with Pam Bondi, because she had personally spied on me and had a document that showcased the files that I had looked at when I was reviewing the Epstein files. And so, I made a — you know, I made a big stink about it. AMY GOODMAN: I mean, this is a big deal, Congressmember Jayapal. Just to explain, for people to understand, you have to go into a room and look at the files, and they were filming you? They were surveilling you looking at the files? REP. PRAMILA JAYAPAL: They were surveilling me, not with film, but what they did is they had a password-protected account, so you had to sign in. And what they did is they downloaded my search history. And I only found out, Amy, because a photographer — I think it was AP photographer — took a picture of her binder when she was testifying, and and it said “Representative Pramila Jayapal’s search history,” and it had all of the files that I had looked at documented on that piece of paper. So, yes, they were surveilling members of Congress and what we searched for, in preparation for her hearing before us on the Judiciary Committee. So, I met with her. It was supposed to be one on one. And, of course, Todd Blanche walks in five minutes after. I spent an hour with both of them, and I grilled them on the surveillance of members of Congress and also on all of the things that they had done to hurt survivors and to protect the president. And I have to tell you, Todd Blanche was responsible for all of that. It was very clear to me that he was the one who was making the decisions, who was acting, essentially, as the president’s personal attorney in redacting the names of powerful predators and allowing for the release of the names of these victims, who — of these survivors. He really, I think, wanted to discourage anyone else from coming forward with more information. He failed at that. They are continuing to come forward. But it’s just another reason why he shouldn’t be — a big reason why he shouldn’t be the attorney general.

WASHINGTON – Acting Attorney General Todd Blanche is denying he privately told senators he “made a mistake” by setting up his department’s $1.8 billion “anti-weaponization” fund to give payouts to Jan. 6 rioters, even as two Democratic senators say he told them directly he regretted setting up the slush fund.

Sen. Dick Durbin (D-Ill.), the top Democrat on the Senate Judiciary Committee, shared this detail with reporters last Tuesday, right after leaving a meeting with Blanche. He and one of his senior aides, who also attended the meeting, even said it was a direct quote.

Advertisement

But buried in more than 400 pages of Blanche’s written responses to questions from the committee, where he’s currently awaiting a vote to be officially confirmed to his post, Blanche denies he ever said that. He submitted his responses to committee members’ questions last Friday, but the panel just made them public this week.

“In our meeting this week, you said, ‘I made a mistake’ by creating this slush fund,” Durbin says on Page 8. “Do you stand by this characterization of this slush fund?”

“I contest the accuracy of this statement,” Blanche replied.

Acting Attorney General Todd Blanche is denying he told Sen. Dick Durbin (D-Ill.) that he "made a mistake" by setting up Trump's $1.8 billion anti-weaponization fund. Hmm. Senate Judiciary Committee

Advertisement

It’s a strange point to dispute, considering Durbin sat down with reporters and discussed this exchange in detail last week. Asked Wednesday about Blanche denying he made those comments, the Illinois senator just chuckled.

“I verified it with several of my staffers who were present,” said Durbin. “I stand by it.”

He speculated that Blanche is publicly disputing that he called Trump’s fund “a mistake” because “that’s not proper MAGA talk.”

Sen. Richard Blumenthal (D-Conn.), also a member of the judiciary panel, said Blanche said the same thing to him, too, when they met privately last week.

Advertisement

“He said it to me in my office,” he told HuffPost. “He acknowledged that it was a mistake. He said, basically, he took the blame for it.”

A Justice Department spokesperson did not immediately respond to a request for comment.

Blanche appears to be trying to have it both ways when he talks about his fund that crashed and burned in Congress: privately distancing himself from it to try to win over Senate votes for his confirmation, while publicly standing by it to keep Trump happy.

Blanche, who previously served as Trump’s personal attorney, created the Justice Department’s fund in May. He issued a memo instructing the Treasury Department to dump $1.776 billion in taxpayer money into an account for payouts to people claiming they were unfairly targeted by the government, effectively setting up a compensation fund for Jan. 6 rioters and the president’s political allies.

Advertisement

The idea for it was borne out of corruption. In January, Trump sued the Treasury Department and the Internal Revenue Service — both agencies he oversees — for $10 billion after a contractor leaked his personal tax information to the press. Instead of heading into litigation, Trump and the Justice Department, which is also under his thumb, ultimately reached a settlement that included the creation of this fund.

But Trump’s efforts to get Congress to appropriate money for it were met with outcry by the public and fierce opposition by lawmakers in both parties. Senate Republicans ultimately forced the president to ditch the fund in June, and Blanche has since called it dead.

The Trump administration, meanwhile, has been finding other ways to pay out Jan. 6 rioters.

On Wednesday, Blumenthal said the reason the slush fund even came up in his meeting with Blanche is because he made the point that Trump thought it “was a great idea.” Blanche responded by saying, in fact, it was his mistake.

topic: Trump Economic Policies Georgia Scoffs Affordability

left.title: Trump scoffs at ‘affordability’ as he touts economic policies at Georgia rally

right.title: Trump Rallies in Georgia, Touting the Trump Accounts, New Policies, and Key Economic Wins

farLeftText: Donald Trump scoffed at the word “affordability” as a political message, describing it at a campaign rally on Wednesday as a term concocted by Democratic political consultants to criticize his presidency. Yet even as the president dismissed the notion of affordability, he devoted much of his speech at Wheeler high school to touting the bread-and-butter benefits of his economic policies. “So I just want you to remember that if you’re paying a little bit more – a lot of it’s come down now, we got it coming down very rapidly actually,” Trump said in Marietta, an Atlanta suburb, casting higher prices today as a lingering effect of the Biden administration. Americans are broadly disappointed with Trump’s handling of the economy, with just 33% of voters approving in a recent Washington Post-Ipsos poll, an assessment that could hurt his party’s hopes of bucking historical precedent and retaining control of Congress in November’s midterm elections. The president was in Georgia, a politically divided state where high-profile contests for governor and Senate are playing out, to promote his newly funded “Trump accounts”, which establish tax-free investment funds for every child born through 2028, seeded with $1,000 from the federal government. Speaking before the president, treasury secretary Scott Bessent described the accounts as the “triumph of capitalism over socialism”, taking a swipe at New York mayor Zohran Mamdani. “Look around our campuses and classrooms today – apart from those here at Wheeler, of course – you will find that our students are being sold the seductive lie that government can solve every problem, redistribute every dollar and supplant its judgment for our own,” Bessent said. “In New York, the current mayor rose to power by repackaging failed ideals of the past as a moral cause of the future. But in Georgia, you know that beneath that youthful veneer lies the oldest impulse in politics which is to concentrate power and then to call it compassion.” Several Republican candidates joined Trump in Marietta, where the president likened US senator Jon Ossoff, a Democrat seeking re-election in one of the most closely watched races of the cycle, to the character Pee-wee Herman, portrayed by the late actor Paul Reubens. Polling shows Ossoff with a lead over the Republican nominee in the Senate race, US congressman Mike Collins, who was in Washington where his vote was needed to help pass a budget bill to fund the Iran war and other White House priorities. Rick Jackson, Georgia’s Republican candidate for governor, pledged to ask the state legislature to match the $1,000 federal contribution to childhood Trump investment accounts if elected. If they failed to do so, Jackson said he would match the figure himself. Jackson is the founder of Jackson Healthcare, a medical staffing company. His personal net worth has been estimated to be more than $3bn. In 2024, the state of Georgia recorded more than 126,000 live births, which equates to $126m in annual contributions for Trump accounts. Georgia began the 2026 fiscal year with an unprecedented budget cushion of about $15bn in the bank, with $9bn in unrestricted funds. This surplus resulted from conservative budgeting that began in 2021 as a reaction to pandemic pressures. That money has become an intense political question, with Republicans looking for ways to provide property tax relief while Democrats arguing for a broader investment in health care and child care support. Parents, friends and employers will be able to deposit as much as $5,000 a year into the Trump accounts, which will be controlled by parents and guardians until their children turn 18, after which the account holder gains control and can use saved-up funds to pay for college, buy a home or start a business. The $1,000 contributions will cost the government $17bn through 2028, according to the Committee for a Responsible Federal Budget. “For decades, Washington politicians gave our children absolutely nothing but debt,” Trump said. “But the Trump accounts, we’re finally seeing and ensuring that every young American gets a great start in life and a really fair shot at the American dream, which is what we all want. They all aspire to that.” Trump increased the national debt by about $8tn in his first term, more than any other president on an inflation-adjusted basis and nearly as much as the government accrued during Barack Obama’s two terms in office. Spending during Trump’s second term is on pace with his first-term mark, with an additional $3bn accrued so far. Inflation currently stands at about 3%, with a decrease of 0.4% last month, driven primarily by a nearly 10% drop in gasoline and oil prices following the short-lived truce in the Iran war. Core inflation was flat for the first time in five years. But gasoline prices have again risen above $4 a gallon in the US, according to AAA tracking, a direct result of oil prices climbing after reawakened hostilities with Iran and the contested strait of Hormuz, a critical waterway for global oil and gas shipments. Trump said he had just come from Dover air force base in Delaware to attend the dignified transfer ceremony for US service members killed in the conflict with Iran. The family of 1st Lt Tyler James Feehan, 25, of Ewa Beach, Hawaii accompanied the president on Air Force One to Georgia and attended the event in Marietta, Trump said. Trump continued to claim that his policies have drawn pledges of $19.2tn into the US economy, a figure that economists from the libertarian Cato Institute and elsewhere consider inflated by illusory investment promises.

topic: Election Deniers Primaries Arizona Trumpbacked Candidates

left.title: Election deniers win Republican primaries in Arizona

center.title: Trump-backed candidates, 2020 election deniers, win Arizona primaries

farLeftText:
Donald Trump-backed candidates in Arizona prevailed in key primary contests on Tuesday, setting up a fall campaign that will test whether Republicans can reverse some of their recent losses in the presidential battleground state.

Andy Biggs, the hard-right congressman endorsed by Donald Trump, easily won the Republican nomination to challenge the incumbent Democrat, Governor Katie Hobbs.

The race is expected to be among the most fiercely contested in the country, with control of the governor’s office carrying outsized national significance in a state that became “ground zero” for election denialism after Trump’s narrow loss there in 2020.



Biggs, a former chair of the House Freedom caucus who was a central figure in the effort to overturn Trump’s election defeat, trounced fellow congressman David Schweikert, by appealing to the party’s deeply conservative base. Hobbs, who flipped the seat from red to blue in 2022, ran unopposed.

Alex Kolodin, a Republican state representative and attorney who helped lead failed legal efforts to overturn Arizona’s 2020 and 2022 election results, also won the party’s primary to become its secretary of state: Arizona’s top election official.

Kolodin will face the Democratic incumbent, Adrian Fontes, in November for the office that will oversee elections in a swing state during the 2028 presidential race. Fontes has cast himself as a bulwark against Trump’s election denialism, while overseeing elections in one of the nation’s most closely watched battleground states.

In an all-caps social media post on Wednesday, Trump declared “A COMPLETE SWEEP!!!”

“MAGA CANDIDATES WON EVERYTHING LAST NIGHT IN ARIZONA,” Trump wrote on Truth Social. He added: “AFTER DECADES, ARIZONA IS BECOMING A REPUBLICAN STATE.”

In fact, Arizona was long a Republican stronghold and only recently transformed into a competitive swing state, fueled by rapid population growth, shifting suburban voting patterns around Phoenix and an influx of college-educated voters.

In the decade since Trump’s 2016 election, Arizona Democrats have gained control of the top three statewide offices – governor, attorney general and secretary of state – as well as both US Senate seats. But Trump still narrowly carried Arizona in 2024 after losing the state four years earlier – a shock defeat he unsuccessfully sought to overturn.

Throughout the Republican gubernatorial campaign, Biggs and Schweikert offered contrasting visions for reclaiming the governorship. Schweikert, who gave up his toss-up congressional seat to run for governor after eight terms, argued that Biggs’s hard-right politics and unswerving embrace of the president had allowed Democrats to make gains in once-ruby-red Arizona. But Republican voters in Arizona, as they have across the country.

View image in fullscreen Alex Kolodin in Phoenix in May. Photograph: Rob Schumacher/The Republic/USA TODAY Network/Reuters Connect

This year, Republicans are contending with unfavorable national political conditions. Historically, the president’s party loses ground in the midterm elections, and Democrats are hopeful they can seize control of the House – and possibly the Senate, riding a wave of discontent over high gas prices and an unpopular Iran war that have dragged down Trump’s approval ratings. Two toss-up House districts in Arizona are key to that effort.

Arizona’s first congressional district, anchored in affluent Scottsdale, has long been a Democratic target as the suburbs shifted away from the Republican party over the past decade.

Two years ago, Schweikert won re-election with just under 52% of the vote, narrowly defeating Amish Shah, a former state lawmaker who Democrats appeared poised to renominate on Tuesday.

His challenger, the former local news anchor Marlene Galán-Woods, also sought the nomination two years ago. This year, Galán-Woods, the widow of Grant Woods, the former Republican attorney general who publicly broke with his party over Trump, earned the controversial backing of the Democratic congressional campaign committee.

View image in fullscreen Katie Hobbs waves to campaign volunteers as voters cast their ballots on primary election day in Phoenix. Photograph: Ross D Franklin/AP

Shah will face Republican Jay Feely, a former Arizona Cardinals kicker, in November. Feely faced Joseph Chaplik, a former state lawmaker, and John Trobough, a technology and telecommunications executive, in his party’s primary.

In the sixth congressional district, which extends from the suburbs of Tucson to the US-Mexico border, encompassing much of south-eastern Arizona, Democrats hope national headwinds facing the president’s party will dislodge Republican congressman Juan Ciscomani, who is seeking a third term. Democrat JoAnna Mendoza, a Marine Corps veteran, was running unopposed.

Meanwhile, the contest to succeed Biggs in the safely Republican fifth congressional district has drawn unusual attention. The Trump-backed Republican nominee, Mark Lamb, the former Pinal county sheriff, spent the closing weeks of the primary campaign confronting allegations of sexual misconduct and abuse of power reported in a multi-part investigation by the Arizona Republic. Prosecutors found no basis for criminal charges, and Trump has reaffirmed his support for Lamb, who has denied wrongdoing.

View image in fullscreen Mark Lamb in San Tan Valley, Arizona, in July 2022. Photograph: Bill Clark/CQ-Roll Call via Getty Images

He will face Democrat Elizabeth Lee, a nurse, in the general election.

Elsewhere, Republicans chose their nominees for two top state positions that Democrats see as critical to pushing back against the Trump administration.

Warren Petersen, president of the Arizona senate, won the Republican primary race to take on Democrat Kris Mayes for attorney general. Mayes, an outspoken critic of Trump, won the office in 2022 by just 280 votes after an automatic recount, making it one of the closest statewide races in Arizona history. He was competing for the nomination against Rodney Glassman, a former Democrat who was the party’s Senate nominee against John McCain in 2010 before joining the Republican party.

Tuesday’s primary also settled tight contests in legislative races across Arizona, where Republicans hold slim majorities in both chambers of the state capitol.

centerText: Trump-backed candidates, 2020 election deniers, win Arizona primaries toggle caption Ross D. Franklin/AP Arizona voters have picked their party nominees for governor and two competitive congressional races, as the primary election outcome provides perspective on how voters are thinking in a key swing state. President Trump-backed candidates prevailed over other Republicans in the race for governor and a key U.S. House seat. GOP voters also chose candidates for governor and secretary of state who have openly opposed the 2020 presidential election results in a state that has been at the center of debates over election security and integrity. U.S. Rep. Andy Biggs is the Republican nominee for governor. After the 2020 election, he refused to certify the results over Trump's unverified claims that the election was stolen for former President Joe Biden. For Arizona's secretary of state, its top elections officer, GOP voters nominated Alexander Kolodin, an attorney who participated in lawsuits challenging the 2020 election results. Sponsor Message Both governor and secretary of state can be key positions in asserting power over the election process in the battleground state. While Trump won the state in 2024, Arizonans elected a Democratic governor two years before that. Trump's popularity in Arizona has also dwindled over his handling of the economy. But that didn't stop Republican voters from picking Trump-endorsed candidates as the nominee in top races. Of the 18 congressional districts across the country that are considered true toss-ups, two of them are in Arizona. Democrats are aiming to flip those two congressional seats from red to blue, both critical battles for the Democratic Party if it wants to have a shot at taking back the House in November. Trump-backed candidate faces Democratic incumbent for the governor's office Republican voters selected a Trump-endorsed conservative, Biggs, to challenge Democratic Gov. Katie Hobbs for the role in November. Biggs beat three other Republican candidates, including a bid from U.S. Rep. David Schweikert, who was considered to be the more pragmatic, moderate candidate. Biggs is a longtime Trump loyalist who was a party leader in the ultra-right House Freedom Caucus. Biggs, though, has attempted to tamper down his conservative persona during the primary election campaign by painting himself as someone who could appeal to independent voters. Sponsor Message On the Democratic side, Hobbs is considered to be a vulnerable candidate. Hobbs narrowly defeated conservative Republican Kari Lake for governor in 2022. Despite her endangered status, Hobbs has experience coalescing swing voters in a race against an ultra-conservative, MAGA-aligned challenger. A Biggs victory appears to indicate that Republican primary voters are swayed by Trump's opinion, even if the candidate may have a tougher time convincing swing voters ahead of the general election. Cook Political Report labeled the race as a toss-up ahead of the primary, but following the results switched its analysis to reflect a slight Democratic advantage. Candidates locked in on key toss-up races The Trump-endorsed candidate, former NFL kicker Jay Feely, won the Republican nomination in one of the nation's most competitive elections. Feely's Democratic challenger hadn't been determined as of late Tuesday night. The wide-open seat in Arizona's 1st Congressional District will help determine which party holds control of the House. Republicans in the district, which consists of the Phoenix metro area, picked the more moderate candidate out of the two front-runners. Feely beat hard-line Republican former state lawmaker Joseph Chaplik, who used to be a member of the Arizona legislature's Freedom Caucus and had won the support of President Trump. On the Democratic side, Amish Shah is leading former broadcast journalist Marlene Galan-Woods, a candidate backed by the Democratic Congressional Campaign Committee. Galan-Woods had been endorsed by Sen. Mark Kelly, one of the most well-known Democrats in the state. She also switched her party affiliation from Republican to Democrat in 2018. But the race is anyone's seat. Trump carried the district by three points in 2024, and Biden won the district by less than two points in 2020. Whoever wins in November will replace outgoing Republican Rep. Schweikert, who lost the GOP nomination for Arizona governor. Another toss-up race is in Arizona's 6th Congressional District. Voters will pick between Republican Rep. Juan Ciscomani and Democrat JoAnna Mendoza in November. Both candidates ran unopposed in their respective primary elections. Sponsor Message Ciscomani was first elected in 2022, but won his reelection bid in 2024 with 50% of the vote. Mendoza is a military veteran who served two tours in Iraq and one in Afghanistan. The district covers parts of Tucson, a sliver of the southern border and rural communities across southeastern Arizona. It's considered by Cook Political Report to be one of the most evenly divided districts in the nation. Trump won the district by less than a point in 2024 and lost by that same margin in 2020. In Arizona's blue-leaning 4th Congressional District, Democratic incumbent Rep. Greg Stanton fended off a primary challenge by progressive candidate Kai Newkirk.

topic: Inter Miami Casemiro Investigation Sign Announces

left.title: Inter Miami sign Casemiro as MLS announces investigation into tampering

center.title: Inter Miami under investigation after signing Casemiro

farLeftText: Inter Miami have signed Casemiro as a free agent, but Major League Soccer is investigating the South Florida side for potential tampering in the process of acquiring the Brazilian midfielder. Casemiro has signed what amounts to a one-year contract with Miami, beginning now and ending at the conclusion of the 2027 “sprint season” that precedes the league’s switch to a fall-to-spring calendar in 2027-28. There is an option for an extension that would see the Brazilian stay in Miami through the 2028-29 MLS season. Both the club and league announcement said that Inter Miami had acquired the discovery rights to Casemiro from the Los Angeles Galaxy. The league did not specify how or under what terms Miami acquired Casemiro’s discovery priority. “Major League Soccer is reviewing a tampering allegation against Inter Miami CF,” the MLS statement said. “The league is gathering all relevant information and will refrain from further comment until the review is complete. While Inter Miami CF and the LA Galaxy have reached a settlement for the Discovery Priority to sign Casemiro, the terms will be released upon conclusion of the tampering investigation.” Casemiro, a five-time Champions League winner with Real Madrid, recently completed a four-season stint at Manchester United. He played with Brazil at the 2026 World Cup as the Seleçao were eliminated by Norway in the last 16. A strong defensive midfielder, Casemiro joins an Inter Miami side already boasting Lionel Messi, Rodrigo De Paul and Luis Suárez as the stars of a team defending their 2025 MLS Cup title win. In MLS, most major player acquisitions from outside the league are handled via the discovery process. In the discovery process, each team maintains a list of up to five players for whom they have priority to sign should they decide to come to the league. However, that does not mean that only that club can sign that player – MLS clubs can work out deals with each other to transfer the discovery rights from one club to the other, enabling the other to enter into negotiations with the player. Marco Reus, for example, was initially on Charlotte FC’s discovery list when the LA Galaxy sought to sign him in 2024, resulting in the Galaxy paying Charlotte $400,000 for Reus’ discovery rights. MLS’s announcement of the investigation into Inter Miami did not specify whether the tampering charge concerned any negotiations Inter Miami may have had with Casemiro directly before acquiring his discovery rights, or whether it was focused more on the acquisition of the discovery rights. The 34-year-old is considered one of the best midfielders of his generation. The São Paulo native moved to Real Madrid in 2013 and went on to win those five Champions League titles in addition to three La Liga crowns and a Copa del Rey. His biggest success with Manchester United came in winning the 2023-24 FA Cup. skip past newsletter promotion Free newsletter | Twice weekly Sign up to Soccer Desk: World Cup Edition Jonathan Wilson and Guardian journalists bring expert analysis and reporting on the biggest stories in soccer. Preview latest Enter your email Sign up after newsletter promotion “Casemiro embodies everything this club stands for: leadership, a winning mentality, and an extraordinary track record at the highest level of the game,” Inter Miami managing owner Jorge Mas said in the club’s announcement. Inter Miami co-owner David Beckham added: “He is a person and a player that I have admired for a long time. He is a winner who has achieved so much in the game and after such an incredible career with Real Madrid and Manchester United I’m delighted that he’s decided to make Miami his next home.”

centerText:
Major League Soccer says it is investigating allegations of tampering by Inter Miami after the club signed Brazil midfielder Casemiro.

On Wednesday, Miami announced they had signed the 34-year-old - who left Manchester United at the end of last season - on a deal that runs to the end of the 2027 MLS campaign, with an option to extend until June 2029.

But moments later, the MLS released a statement saying it was "reviewing a tampering allegation" against the club.

"The league is gathering all relevant information and will refrain from further comment until the review is complete," it added.

"While Inter Miami CF and the LA Galaxy have reached a settlement for the discovery priority [first rights] to sign Casemiro, the terms will be released upon conclusion of the tampering investigation."

The investigation concerns Casemiro's discovery rights, an MLS regulation that gives a specific club first refusal over a player should they want to join.

A club may have up to five players on their discovery list at any time and can add or remove players.

Los Angeles Galaxy held the discovery rights for Casemiro but in announcing the player's signing, Inter Miami said they had acquired the rights from the Galaxy.

LA Galaxy said in a statement: "The LA Galaxy and Inter Miami CF have reached a settlement for the discovery priority to sign Casemiro, and the terms will be released upon conclusion of the tampering investigation currently open with Major League Soccer."

Casemiro announced in January that he would depart Old Trafford at the end of the 2026-27 season following a mutual decision not to renew his contract.

He won five Champions League titles with Real Madrid before moving to United in 2022, and played for Brazil as they reached the last 16 at this summer's World Cup.

"What motivates me the most, and I think this is true for every player, is winning and continuing to grow," he said.

Casemiro made 160 appearances for United, scoring 26 goals and providing 14 assists as he won one EFL Cup and one FA Cup.

In the wake of Casemiro's departure, United have prioritised midfield reinforcements in this window, buying Andrey Santos for £50m from Chelsea, and Youri Tielemans from Aston Villa for £35m.

Before the announcement of the investigation, Inter Miami co-owner Sir David Beckham said of Casemiro: "He is a winner who has achieved so much in the game and after such an incredible career with Real Madrid and Manchester United I'm delighted that he's decided to make Miami his next home."

At Miami, Casemiro is set to join former Real and Manchester United team-mate Sergio Reguilon as well as Argentina's World Cup finalists Lionel Messi and Rodrigo de Paul.

It is not the first time Inter Miami have been probed over transfer dealings.

In 2021 they were fined $2m after breaking MLS rules with the signing of France midfielder Blaise Matuidi from Juventus the previous year.

The MLS said Matuidi had been paid more than was allowed, effectively giving the team four "designated players" last season rather than the permitted three. Designated players' wages do not count towards the MLS salary cap.

Known as the 'Beckham rule', it was introduced when the ex-England captain joined Los Angeles Galaxy in 2007.

topic: Know Nolan Wells Death Mississippi Independent

left-center.title: What to Know About the Death of Nolan Wells in Mississippi

center.title: What to know about the Nolan Wells independent autopsy results

farLeftText: No article found in this category for this topic.
centerLeftText: No article found in this category for this topic.
centerText:
A preliminary independent autopsy for Nolan Wells released Wednesday could not provide an explanation for his death, and called for further investigation.

The autopsy, conducted by forensic pathologist Dr. Roger Mitchell on July 10, did not determine the manner or cause of death for the Black teen. Wells was found dead after going missing on a Mississippi island on July 4.

"This pathologist cannot rule out if any intervening non-accidental factors contributed to or caused the death of Nolan Wells," civil rights attorney Ben Crump said Wednesday at the announcement, reading from the autopsy report.



Watch the video in the player above.

"We have more questions than answers at this time," said Crump, who is representing Wells' family.

WATCH: Family of Nolan Wells expected to announce results of independent autopsy

The autopsy found red discoloration in the soft tissue at the back of Wells' head. Crump said this finding needed further investigation because Mitchell had reported that the cause of the discoloration was unclear.

What the autopsy found, and what is unknown

Mitchell, president of the National Medical Association and former chief medical examiner of the District of Columbia, did not attend the news conference, held at the 117th NAACP National Convention in Chicago. He wrote that he did not receive Wells' internal neck structures, including his trachea. Mitchell said he didn't know why the initial pathologist kept the neck structures, but added that it is not uncommon to keep these body parts for additional testing if there are concerns of fracture or neck compression.

READ MORE: What we know about death of Nolan Wells, Black teen who went missing on Mississippi boat trip

However, in the absence of those body parts, Mitchell said he "cannot rule out injury to the neck as a contributor or a cause of death."

The most significant limitation to conducting a second autopsy, according to the report, "was the inability to directly observe the airway and the lungs for water and directly observe the stomach for gastric contents."

The initial autopsy removed and dissected Wells' organs, as is customary. After the second autopsy, which was paid for by civil rights activist Colin Kaepernick, Mitchell listed seven outstanding questions, including what led Wells to enter the water, whether there was water in his airway and whether he had a brain bleed.

"The condition of Nolan Wells before he entered the water would be the crux of the matter," Crump said. "It would be of utmost importance to being able to determine a cause and manner of death."

What Wells' family and attorney have said

Crump demanded transparency from local authorities, saying he hopes the Mississippi medical examiner's office provides Mitchell with its photos and observations of Wells' body. The Jackson County Sheriff's Department's autopsy and toxicology results have still not been made public.

WATCH: Nolan Wells' family presses for transparent investigation into his death

Crump also said that Mitchell believed the proper venue to interpret the results of the toxicology report would be before a grand jury.

"We would hope that the district attorney would present Dr. Mitchell before the grand jury as well. We want all relevant information to be heard by the grand jury," Crump said.

Wells' mother Christine Wells-Wonsley said "it's heartbreaking and it's concerning" to not have heard from local authorities. She said the last time she spoke to the sheriff's office was a day or two after Wells' body was found on July 6.

Wells-Wonsley said she and her husband are "moving the way we are" because local authorities said they believed Wells had drowned and did not suspect foul play "not even an hour" after his body was removed.

In a July 10 news conference, Crump described "glaring contradictions" in witness accounts of Wells' disappearance, related to whether he stayed on or left the island and why he didn't have his phone with him, and questioned the idea that a young athlete who knew how to swim would drown.

The Rev. Al Sharpton, filmmaker Tyler Perry and former NFL player Terrell Owens are offering a $125,000 reward for any information leading to a conviction in Wells' death.

"The only question that we have is what happened to our son," Wells-Wonsley said.
